title: Planets

infobox.value: is a large, [rounded](/wiki/Hydrostatic_equilibrium "Hydrostatic equilibrium") [astronomical body](/wiki/Astronomical_object "Astronomical object") that is generally required to be in [orbit](/wiki/Orbit "Orbit") around a [star](/wiki/Star "Star"), [stellar remnant](/wiki/Stellar_remnant "Stellar remnant"), or [brown dwarf](/wiki/Brown_dwarf "Brown dwarf"), and is not one itself.[[1]](#cite_note-exodef-1) The [Solar System](/wiki/Solar_System "Solar System") has eight planets by the most restrictive definition of the term: the [terrestrial planets](/wiki/Terrestrial_planet "Terrestrial planet") [Mercury](/wiki/Mercury_(planet) "Mercury (planet)"), [Venus](/wiki/Venus "Venus"), [Earth](/wiki/Earth "Earth"), and [Mars](/wiki/Mars "Mars"), and the [giant planets](/wiki/Giant_planet "Giant planet") [Jupiter](/wiki/Jupiter "Jupiter"), [Saturn](/wiki/Saturn "Saturn"), [Uranus](/wiki/Uranus "Uranus"), and [Neptune](/wiki/Neptune "Neptune"). The best available theory of planet formation is the [nebular hypothesis](/wiki/Nebular_hypothesis "Nebular hypothesis"), which posits that an [interstellar cloud](/wiki/Interstellar_cloud "Interstellar cloud") collapses out of a [nebula](/wiki/Nebula "Nebula") to create a young [protostar](/wiki/Protostar "Protostar") orbited by a [protoplanetary disk](/wiki/Protoplanetary_disk "Protoplanetary disk"). Planets grow in this disk by the gradual accumulation of material driven by [gravity](/wiki/Gravity "Gravity"), a process called [accretion](/wiki/Accretion_(astrophysics) "Accretion (astrophysics)").

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Planets](/w/index.php?title=Planets&redirect=no "Planets"))

Large, round non-stellar astronomical object

This article is about the astronomical object. For other uses, see [Planet (disambiguation)](/wiki/Planet_(disambiguation) "Planet (disambiguation)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cf/Planet_collage_to_scale.jpg/500px-Planet_collage_to_scale.jpg)](/wiki/File:Planet_collage_to_scale.jpg)

The eight planets of the [Solar System](/wiki/Solar_System "Solar System") with size to scale (up to down, left to right): [Saturn](/wiki/Saturn "Saturn"), [Jupiter](/wiki/Jupiter "Jupiter"), [Uranus](/wiki/Uranus "Uranus"), [Neptune](/wiki/Neptune "Neptune") (outer planets), [Earth](/wiki/Earth "Earth"), [Venus](/wiki/Venus "Venus"), [Mars](/wiki/Mars "Mars"), and [Mercury](/wiki/Mercury_(planet) "Mercury (planet)") (inner planets)

A **planet** is a large, [rounded](/wiki/Hydrostatic_equilibrium "Hydrostatic equilibrium") [astronomical body](/wiki/Astronomical_object "Astronomical object") that is generally required to be in [orbit](/wiki/Orbit "Orbit") around a [star](/wiki/Star "Star"), [stellar remnant](/wiki/Stellar_remnant "Stellar remnant"), or [brown dwarf](/wiki/Brown_dwarf "Brown dwarf"), and is not one itself.[[1]](#cite_note-exodef-1) The [Solar System](/wiki/Solar_System "Solar System") has eight planets by the most restrictive definition of the term: the [terrestrial planets](/wiki/Terrestrial_planet "Terrestrial planet") [Mercury](/wiki/Mercury_(planet) "Mercury (planet)"), [Venus](/wiki/Venus "Venus"), [Earth](/wiki/Earth "Earth"), and [Mars](/wiki/Mars "Mars"), and the [giant planets](/wiki/Giant_planet "Giant planet") [Jupiter](/wiki/Jupiter "Jupiter"), [Saturn](/wiki/Saturn "Saturn"), [Uranus](/wiki/Uranus "Uranus"), and [Neptune](/wiki/Neptune "Neptune"). The best available theory of planet formation is the [nebular hypothesis](/wiki/Nebular_hypothesis "Nebular hypothesis"), which posits that an [interstellar cloud](/wiki/Interstellar_cloud "Interstellar cloud") collapses out of a [nebula](/wiki/Nebula "Nebula") to create a young [protostar](/wiki/Protostar "Protostar") orbited by a [protoplanetary disk](/wiki/Protoplanetary_disk "Protoplanetary disk"). Planets grow in this disk by the gradual accumulation of material driven by [gravity](/wiki/Gravity "Gravity"), a process called [accretion](/wiki/Accretion_(astrophysics) "Accretion (astrophysics)").

The word *planet* comes from the Greek [πλανήται](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%CF%80%CE%BB%CE%B1%CE%BD%CE%AE%CF%84%CE%B7%CF%82#Ancient_Greek "wikt:πλανήτης") (*planḗtai*) 'wanderers'. In [antiquity](/wiki/Classical_antiquity "Classical antiquity"), this word referred to the [Sun](/wiki/Sun "Sun"), [Moon](/wiki/Moon "Moon"), and five points of light visible to the naked eye that moved across the background of the stars—namely, Mercury, Venus, Mars, Jupiter, and Saturn. Planets have historically had religious associations: [multiple cultures](/wiki/History_of_astronomy#Early_History "History of astronomy") identified celestial bodies with gods, and these connections with mythology and [folklore](/wiki/Folklore "Folklore") persist in the schemes for naming newly discovered Solar System bodies. Earth itself was recognized as a planet when [heliocentrism](/wiki/Heliocentrism "Heliocentrism") supplanted [geocentrism](/wiki/Geocentrism "Geocentrism") during the 16th and 17th centuries.

With the development of the [telescope](/wiki/Telescope "Telescope"), the meaning of *planet* broadened to include objects only visible with assistance: the [moons](/wiki/Natural_satellite "Natural satellite") of the planets beyond Earth; the [ice giants](/wiki/Ice_giant "Ice giant") Uranus and Neptune; [Ceres](/wiki/Ceres_(dwarf_planet) "Ceres (dwarf planet)") and other bodies later recognized to be part of the [asteroid belt](/wiki/Asteroid_belt "Asteroid belt"); and [Pluto](/wiki/Pluto_(dwarf_planet) "Pluto (dwarf planet)"), later found to be the largest member of the collection of icy bodies known as the [Kuiper belt](/wiki/Kuiper_belt "Kuiper belt"). The discovery of other large objects in the Kuiper belt, particularly [Eris](/wiki/Eris_(dwarf_planet) "Eris (dwarf planet)"), spurred debate about how exactly to define a planet. In 2006, the [International Astronomical Union](/wiki/International_Astronomical_Union "International Astronomical Union") (IAU) adopted a definition of a planet in the Solar System, placing the four terrestrial planets and the four giant planets in the planet category; Ceres, Pluto, and Eris are in the category of [dwarf planet](/wiki/Dwarf_planet "Dwarf planet").[[2]](#cite_note-IAU-2)[[3]](#cite_note-WSGESP-3)[[4]](#cite_note-planetarysociety-4) Many [planetary scientists](/wiki/Planetary_science "Planetary science") have nonetheless continued to apply the term *planet* more broadly, including dwarf planets as well as rounded satellites like the Moon.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

Further advances in astronomy led to the discovery of over 5,900 planets outside the Solar System, termed [exoplanets](/wiki/Exoplanet "Exoplanet"). These often show unusual features that the Solar System planets do not show, such as [hot Jupiters](/wiki/Hot_Jupiter "Hot Jupiter")—giant planets that orbit close to their parent stars, like [51 Pegasi b](/wiki/51_Pegasi_b "51 Pegasi b")—and extremely [eccentric orbits](/wiki/Orbital_eccentricity "Orbital eccentricity"), such as [HD 20782 b](/wiki/HD_20782_b "HD 20782 b"). The discovery of brown dwarfs and planets larger than Jupiter also spurred debate on the definition, regarding where exactly to draw the line between a planet and a star. Multiple exoplanets have been found to orbit in the [habitable zones](/wiki/Circumstellar_habitable_zone "Circumstellar habitable zone") of their stars (where liquid water can potentially exist on a [planetary surface](/wiki/Planetary_surface "Planetary surface")), but Earth remains the only planet known to support [life](/wiki/Life "Life"). Such a habitable zone is complicated by many factors, such as the atmospheric composition (if there is an atmosphere) of the planet; Mars, for example, has the temperature during the day to form liquid water on its surface on the equator, if only the pressure were higher.

## Formation

Main article: [Nebular hypothesis](/wiki/Nebular_hypothesis "Nebular hypothesis")

Artists' impressions

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2d/The_Mysterious_Case_of_the_Disappearing_Dust.jpg/250px-The_Mysterious_Case_of_the_Disappearing_Dust.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Mysterious_Case_of_the_Disappearing_Dust.jpg)

A protoplanetary disk

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/03/PIA18469-AsteroidCollision-NearStarNGC2547-ID8-2013.jpg/250px-PIA18469-AsteroidCollision-NearStarNGC2547-ID8-2013.jpg)](/wiki/File:PIA18469-AsteroidCollision-NearStarNGC2547-ID8-2013.jpg)

Protoplanets colliding during planet formation

It is not known with certainty how planets are formed. The prevailing theory is that they coalesce during the collapse of a [nebula](/wiki/Nebula "Nebula") into a thin disk of gas and dust. A [protostar](/wiki/Protostar "Protostar") forms at the core, surrounded by a rotating [protoplanetary disk](/wiki/Protoplanetary_disk "Protoplanetary disk"). Through [accretion](/wiki/Accretion_(astrophysics) "Accretion (astrophysics)") (a process of sticky collision) dust particles in the disk steadily accumulate [mass](/wiki/Mass "Mass") to form ever-larger bodies. Local concentrations of mass known as [planetesimals](/wiki/Planetesimal "Planetesimal") form, and these accelerate the accretion process by drawing in additional material by their gravitational attraction. These concentrations become increasingly dense until they collapse inward under gravity to form [protoplanets](/wiki/Protoplanet "Protoplanet").[[6]](#cite_note-6) After a planet reaches a mass somewhat larger than Mars's mass, it begins to accumulate an extended [atmosphere](/wiki/Atmosphere "Atmosphere"),[[7]](#cite_note-dangelo_bodenheimer_2013-7) greatly increasing the capture rate of the planetesimals by means of [atmospheric drag](/wiki/Drag_(physics) "Drag (physics)").[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-dangelo2014-9) Depending on the accretion history of solids and gas, a [giant planet](/wiki/Giant_planet "Giant planet"), an [ice giant](/wiki/Ice_giant "Ice giant"), or a [terrestrial planet](/wiki/Terrestrial_planet "Terrestrial planet") may result.[[10]](#cite_note-lhdb2009-10)[[11]](#cite_note-ddl2011-11)[[12]](#cite_note-chambes2011-12) It is thought that the [regular satellites](/wiki/Regular_satellite "Regular satellite") of Jupiter, Saturn, and Uranus formed in a similar way;[[13]](#cite_note-arxiv0812-13)[[14]](#cite_note-dangelo_podolak_2015-14) however, [Triton](/wiki/Triton_(moon) "Triton (moon)") was likely [captured](/wiki/Gravitational_capture "Gravitational capture") by Neptune,[[15]](#cite_note-Agnor06-15) and Earth's Moon[[16]](#cite_note-taylor1998-16) and Pluto's Charon might have formed in collisions.[[17]](#cite_note-Stern_2015-17)

When the protostar has grown such that it ignites to form a star, the surviving disk is removed from the inside outward by [photoevaporation](/wiki/Photoevaporation "Photoevaporation"), the [solar wind](/wiki/Solar_wind "Solar wind"), [Poynting–Robertson drag](/wiki/Poynting%E2%80%93Robertson_effect "Poynting–Robertson effect") and other effects.[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19) Thereafter there still may be many protoplanets orbiting the star or each other, but over time many will collide, either to form a larger, combined protoplanet or release material for other protoplanets to absorb.[[20]](#cite_note-20) Those objects that have become massive enough will capture most matter in their orbital neighbourhoods to become planets. Protoplanets that have avoided collisions may become [natural satellites](/wiki/Natural_satellite "Natural satellite") of planets through a process of gravitational capture, or remain in belts of other objects to become either dwarf planets or [small bodies](/wiki/Small_Solar_System_body "Small Solar System body").[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/19/15-044a-SuperNovaRemnant-PlanetFormation-SOFIA-20150319.jpg/250px-15-044a-SuperNovaRemnant-PlanetFormation-SOFIA-20150319.jpg)](/wiki/File:15-044a-SuperNovaRemnant-PlanetFormation-SOFIA-20150319.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/15-044b-SuperNovaRemnant-PlanetFormation-SOFIA-20150319.jpg/250px-15-044b-SuperNovaRemnant-PlanetFormation-SOFIA-20150319.jpg)](/wiki/File:15-044b-SuperNovaRemnant-PlanetFormation-SOFIA-20150319.jpg)

[Supernova remnant](/wiki/Supernova_remnant "Supernova remnant") ejecta producing planet-forming material

The energetic impacts of the smaller planetesimals (as well as [radioactive decay](/wiki/Radioactive_decay "Radioactive decay")) will heat up the growing planet, causing it to at least partially melt. The interior of the planet begins to differentiate by density, with higher density materials sinking toward the [core](/wiki/Planetary_core "Planetary core").[[23]](#cite_note-23) Smaller terrestrial planets lose most of their atmospheres because of this accretion, but the lost gases can be replaced by outgassing from the [mantle](/wiki/Mantle_(geology) "Mantle (geology)") and from the subsequent impact of [comets](/wiki/Comet "Comet")[[24]](#cite_note-24) (smaller planets will lose any atmosphere they gain through various [escape mechanisms](/wiki/Atmospheric_escape "Atmospheric escape")[[25]](#cite_note-25)).

With the discovery and observation of [planetary systems](/wiki/Planetary_system "Planetary system") around stars other than the Sun, it is becoming possible to elaborate, revise or even replace this account. The level of [metallicity](/wiki/Metallicity "Metallicity")—an astronomical term describing the abundance of [chemical elements](/wiki/Chemical_element "Chemical element") with an [atomic number](/wiki/Atomic_number "Atomic number") greater than 2 ([helium](/wiki/Helium "Helium"))—appears to determine the likelihood that a star will have planets.[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27) Hence, a metal-rich [population I star](/wiki/Population_I_star "Population I star") is more likely to have a substantial planetary system than a metal-poor, [population II star](/wiki/Population_II_star "Population II star").[[28]](#cite_note-28)

## Planets in the Solar System

Main article: [Solar System](/wiki/Solar_System "Solar System")

According to the [IAU definition](/wiki/IAU_definition_of_planet "IAU definition of planet"), there are eight planets in the Solar System, which are (in increasing distance from the Sun):[[2]](#cite_note-IAU-2) Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, and Neptune. Jupiter is the largest, at 318 [Earth masses](/wiki/Earth_mass "Earth mass"), whereas Mercury is the smallest, at 0.055 Earth masses.[[29]](#cite_note-JPL-physical-parameters-29)

The planets of the Solar System can be divided into categories based on their composition. [Terrestrials](/wiki/Terrestrial_planet "Terrestrial planet") are similar to Earth, with bodies largely composed of [rock](/wiki/Rock_(geology) "Rock (geology)") and metal: Mercury, Venus, Earth, and Mars. Earth is the largest terrestrial planet.[[30]](#cite_note-Lewis59-30) [Giant planets](/wiki/Giant_planet "Giant planet") are significantly more massive than the terrestrials: Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, and Neptune.[[30]](#cite_note-Lewis59-30) They differ from the terrestrial planets in composition. The [gas giants](/wiki/Gas_giant "Gas giant"), Jupiter and Saturn, are primarily composed of [hydrogen](/wiki/Hydrogen "Hydrogen") and helium and are the most massive planets in the Solar System. Saturn is one third as massive as Jupiter, at 95 Earth masses.[[31]](#cite_note-M19-31) The [ice giants](/wiki/Ice_giant "Ice giant"), Uranus and Neptune, are primarily composed of low-boiling-point materials such as water, [methane](/wiki/Methane "Methane"), and [ammonia](/wiki/Ammonia "Ammonia"), with thick atmospheres of hydrogen and helium. They have a significantly lower mass than the gas giants (only 14 and 17 Earth masses).[[31]](#cite_note-M19-31)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/Solar_System_true_color_%28captions%29.jpg/960px-Solar_System_true_color_%28captions%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Solar_System_true_color_(captions).jpg)

The Sun's, planets', dwarf planets' and moons' size to scale, labelled. Distance of objects is not to scale. The asteroid belt lies between the orbits of Mars and Jupiter, the Kuiper belt lies beyond Neptune's orbit.

Dwarf planets are gravitationally rounded, but have not cleared their orbits of other [bodies](/wiki/Small_Solar_System_body "Small Solar System body"). In increasing order of average distance from the Sun, the ones generally agreed among astronomers are [Ceres](/wiki/Ceres_(dwarf_planet) "Ceres (dwarf planet)"), [Orcus](/wiki/Orcus_(dwarf_planet) "Orcus (dwarf planet)"), [Pluto](/wiki/Pluto "Pluto"), [Haumea](/wiki/Haumea "Haumea"), [Quaoar](/wiki/Quaoar "Quaoar"), [Makemake](/wiki/Makemake "Makemake"), [Gonggong](/wiki/Gonggong_(dwarf_planet) "Gonggong (dwarf planet)"), [Eris](/wiki/Eris_(dwarf_planet) "Eris (dwarf planet)"), and [Sedna](/wiki/Sedna_(dwarf_planet) "Sedna (dwarf planet)").[[32]](#cite_note-Grundy2019-32)[[33]](#cite_note-JWST-33) Ceres is the largest object in the [asteroid belt](/wiki/Asteroid_belt "Asteroid belt"), located between the orbits of Mars and Jupiter. The other eight all orbit beyond Neptune. Orcus, Pluto, Haumea, Quaoar, and Makemake orbit in the [Kuiper belt](/wiki/Kuiper_belt "Kuiper belt"), which is a second belt of small Solar System bodies beyond the orbit of Neptune. Gonggong and Eris orbit in the [scattered disc](/wiki/Scattered_disc "Scattered disc"), which is somewhat further out and, unlike the Kuiper belt, is unstable towards interactions with Neptune. Sedna is the largest known [detached object](/wiki/Detached_object "Detached object"), a population that never comes close enough to the Sun to interact with any of the classical planets; the origins of their orbits are still being debated. All nine are similar to terrestrial planets in having a solid surface, but they are made of ice and rock rather than rock and metal. Moreover, all of them are smaller than Mercury, with Pluto being the largest known dwarf planet and Eris being the most massive.[[34]](#cite_note-Brown_Schaller_2007-34)[[35]](#cite_note-35)

There are at least nineteen [planetary-mass moons](/wiki/Planetary-mass_moon "Planetary-mass moon") or satellite planets—moons large enough to take on ellipsoidal shapes:[[4]](#cite_note-planetarysociety-4)

* One satellite of Earth: the [Moon](/wiki/Moon "Moon")
* Four [satellites of Jupiter](/wiki/Moons_of_Jupiter "Moons of Jupiter"): [Io](/wiki/Io_(moon) "Io (moon)"), [Europa](/wiki/Europa_(moon) "Europa (moon)"), [Ganymede](/wiki/Ganymede_(moon) "Ganymede (moon)"), and [Callisto](/wiki/Callisto_(moon) "Callisto (moon)")
* Seven [satellites of Saturn](/wiki/Moons_of_Saturn "Moons of Saturn"): [Mimas](/wiki/Mimas "Mimas"), [Enceladus](/wiki/Enceladus "Enceladus"), [Tethys](/wiki/Tethys_(moon) "Tethys (moon)"), [Dione](/wiki/Dione_(moon) "Dione (moon)"), [Rhea](/wiki/Rhea_(moon) "Rhea (moon)"), [Titan](/wiki/Titan_(moon) "Titan (moon)"), and [Iapetus](/wiki/Iapetus_(moon) "Iapetus (moon)")
* Five [satellites of Uranus](/wiki/Moons_of_Uranus "Moons of Uranus"): [Miranda](/wiki/Miranda_(moon) "Miranda (moon)"), [Ariel](/wiki/Ariel_(moon) "Ariel (moon)"), [Umbriel](/wiki/Umbriel "Umbriel"), [Titania](/wiki/Titania_(moon) "Titania (moon)"), and [Oberon](/wiki/Oberon_(moon) "Oberon (moon)")
* One [satellite of Neptune](/wiki/Moons_of_Neptune "Moons of Neptune"): [Triton](/wiki/Triton_(moon) "Triton (moon)")
* One [satellite of Pluto](/wiki/Moons_of_Pluto "Moons of Pluto"): [Charon](/wiki/Charon_(moon) "Charon (moon)")

The Moon, Io, and Europa have compositions similar to the terrestrial planets; the others are made of ice and rock like the dwarf planets, with [Tethys](/wiki/Tethys_(moon) "Tethys (moon)") being made of almost pure ice. Europa is often considered an icy planet, though, because its surface ice layer makes it difficult to study its interior.[[4]](#cite_note-planetarysociety-4)[[36]](#cite_note-36) Ganymede and Titan are larger than Mercury by radius, and Callisto almost equals it, but all three are much less massive. Mimas is the smallest object generally agreed to be a [geophysical planet](/wiki/Geophysical_definition_of_planet "Geophysical definition of planet"), at about six millionths of Earth's mass, though there are many larger bodies that may not be geophysical planets (e.g. [Salacia](/wiki/120347_Salacia "120347 Salacia")).[[32]](#cite_note-Grundy2019-32)

## Exoplanets

Main article: [Exoplanet](/wiki/Exoplanet "Exoplanet")

[![Exoplanet detections per year](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9c/Exoplanet_detections_per_year.png/330px-Exoplanet_detections_per_year.png)](/wiki/File:Exoplanet_detections_per_year.png)

Exoplanet detections per year as of August 2023 (by [NASA Exoplanet Archive](/wiki/NASA_Exoplanet_Archive "NASA Exoplanet Archive"))[[37]](#cite_note-37)

An [exoplanet](/wiki/Exoplanet "Exoplanet") is a planet outside the Solar System. As of 26 February 2026, there are 6,256 confirmed [exoplanets](/wiki/Exoplanet "Exoplanet") in 4,664 [planetary systems](/wiki/Planetary_system "Planetary system"), with 1,050 systems [having more than one planet](/wiki/List_of_multiplanetary_systems "List of multiplanetary systems").[[38]](#cite_note-Exoplanet_Catalog_(EPE)-38) Known exoplanets range in size from gas giants [about twice as large as Jupiter](/wiki/WASP-17b "WASP-17b") down to [just over the size of the Moon](/wiki/Kepler-37b "Kepler-37b"). Analysis of [gravitational microlensing](/wiki/Gravitational_microlensing "Gravitational microlensing") data suggests a minimum average of 1.6 bound planets for every star in the [Milky Way](/wiki/Milky_Way "Milky Way").[[39]](#cite_note-nature.com-39)

In early 1992, radio astronomers [Aleksander Wolszczan](/wiki/Aleksander_Wolszczan "Aleksander Wolszczan") and [Dale Frail](/wiki/Dale_Frail "Dale Frail") announced the discovery of two planets orbiting the [pulsar](/wiki/Pulsar "Pulsar") [PSR 1257+12](/wiki/PSR_1257%2B12 "PSR 1257+12").[[40]](#cite_note-Wolszczan-40) This discovery was confirmed and is generally considered to be the first definitive detection of exoplanets. Researchers suspect they formed from a disk remnant left over from the [supernova](/wiki/Supernova "Supernova") that produced the pulsar.[[41]](#cite_note-41)

The first confirmed discovery of an exoplanet orbiting an ordinary [main-sequence](/wiki/Main_sequence "Main sequence") star occurred on 6 October 1995, when [Michel Mayor](/wiki/Michel_Mayor "Michel Mayor") and [Didier Queloz](/wiki/Didier_Queloz "Didier Queloz") of the [University of Geneva](/wiki/University_of_Geneva "University of Geneva") announced the detection of [51 Pegasi b](/wiki/51_Pegasi_b "51 Pegasi b"), an exoplanet around [51 Pegasi](/wiki/51_Pegasi "51 Pegasi").[[42]](#cite_note-whatworlds-42) From then until the [Kepler space telescope](/wiki/Kepler_space_telescope "Kepler space telescope") mission, most of the known exoplanets were gas giants comparable in mass to Jupiter or larger as they were more easily detected. The [catalog of Kepler candidate planets](/wiki/List_of_exoplanets_discovered_using_the_Kepler_space_telescope "List of exoplanets discovered using the Kepler space telescope") consists mostly of planets the size of Neptune and smaller, down to smaller than Mercury.[[43]](#cite_note-43)[[44]](#cite_note-Barclay-2013-44)

In 2011, the [Kepler space telescope](/wiki/Kepler_space_telescope "Kepler space telescope") team reported the discovery of the first Earth-sized exoplanets orbiting a [Sun-like star](/wiki/Solar_analog "Solar analog"), [Kepler-20e](/wiki/Kepler-20e "Kepler-20e") and [Kepler-20f](/wiki/Kepler-20f "Kepler-20f").[[45]](#cite_note-NASA-20111220-45)[[46]](#cite_note-Nature-20111220-46)[[47]](#cite_note-NYT-20111220-47) Since that time, more than 100 planets have been identified that are approximately the [same size as Earth](/wiki/Earth_radius "Earth radius"), 20 of which orbit in the [habitable zone](/wiki/Circumstellar_habitable_zone "Circumstellar habitable zone") of their star—the range of orbits where a terrestrial planet could sustain liquid water on its surface, given enough atmospheric pressure.[[48]](#cite_note-kopparapu-2013-48)[[49]](#cite_note-49)[[50]](#cite_note-50) One in five Sun-like stars is thought to have an Earth-sized planet in its habitable zone, which suggests that the nearest would be expected to be within 12 [light-years](/wiki/Light-year "Light-year") distance from Earth.[[a]](#cite_note-1in5earthsized-53) The frequency of occurrence of such terrestrial planets is one of the variables in the [Drake equation](/wiki/Drake_equation "Drake equation"), which estimates the number of [intelligent, communicating civilizations](/wiki/Extraterrestrial_life "Extraterrestrial life") that exist in the Milky Way.[[53]](#cite_note-54)

There are types of planets that do not exist in the Solar System: [super-Earths](/wiki/Super-Earth "Super-Earth") and [mini-Neptunes](/wiki/Mini-Neptune "Mini-Neptune"), which have masses between that of Earth and Neptune. Objects less than about twice the mass of Earth are expected to be rocky like Earth; beyond that, they become a mixture of volatiles and gas like Neptune.[[54]](#cite_note-ChenKipping-55) The planet [Gliese 581c](/wiki/Gliese_581c "Gliese 581c"), with a mass 5.5–10.4 times the mass of Earth,[[55]](#cite_note-56) attracted attention upon its discovery for potentially being in the habitable zone,[[56]](#cite_note-BBC1-57) though later studies concluded that it is actually too close to its star to be habitable.[[57]](#cite_note-blo07-58) Planets more massive than Jupiter are also known, extending seamlessly into the realm of brown dwarfs.[[58]](#cite_note-Hatzes-59)

Exoplanets have been found that are much closer to their parent star than any planet in the Solar System is to the Sun. Mercury, the closest planet to the Sun at 0.4 [AU](/wiki/Astronomical_unit "Astronomical unit"), takes 88 days for an orbit, but [ultra-short period planets](/wiki/Ultra-short_period_planet "Ultra-short period planet") can orbit in less than a day. The [Kepler-11](/wiki/Kepler-11 "Kepler-11") system has five of its planets in shorter orbits than Mercury's, all of them much more massive than Mercury. There are [hot Jupiters](/wiki/Hot_Jupiter "Hot Jupiter"), such as 51 Pegasi b,[[42]](#cite_note-whatworlds-42) that orbit very close to their star and may evaporate to become [chthonian planets](/wiki/Chthonian_planet "Chthonian planet"), which are the leftover cores. There are also exoplanets that are much farther from their star. Neptune is 30 AU from the Sun and takes 165 years to orbit, but there are exoplanets that are thousands of AU from their star and take more than a million years to orbit (e.g. [COCONUTS-2b](/wiki/COCONUTS-2b "COCONUTS-2b")).[[59]](#cite_note-60)

## Attributes

Although each planet has unique physical characteristics, a number of broad commonalities do exist among them. Some of these characteristics, such as [rings](/wiki/Ring_system "Ring system") or natural satellites, have only as yet been observed in planets in the Solar System, whereas others are commonly observed in exoplanets.[[60]](#cite_note-61)

### Dynamic characteristics

#### Orbit

Main articles: [Orbit](/wiki/Orbit "Orbit") and [orbital elements](/wiki/Orbital_elements "Orbital elements")

See also: [Kepler's laws of planetary motion](/wiki/Kepler%27s_laws_of_planetary_motion "Kepler's laws of planetary motion") and [Exoplanetology § Orbital parameters](/wiki/Exoplanetology#Orbital_parameters "Exoplanetology")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/TheKuiperBelt_Orbits_Pluto_Ecliptic.svg/500px-TheKuiperBelt_Orbits_Pluto_Ecliptic.svg.png)](/wiki/File:TheKuiperBelt_Orbits_Pluto_Ecliptic.svg)

The orbit of the planet Neptune compared to that of [Pluto](/wiki/Pluto "Pluto"). Note the elongation of Pluto's orbit in relation to Neptune's ([eccentricity](/wiki/Orbital_eccentricity "Orbital eccentricity")), as well as its large angle to the ecliptic ([inclination](/wiki/Inclination "Inclination")).

In the Solar System, all the planets orbit the Sun in the same direction as the [Sun rotates](/wiki/Solar_rotation "Solar rotation"): [counter-clockwise](/wiki/Counter-clockwise "Counter-clockwise") as seen from above the Sun's north pole. At least one exoplanet, [WASP-17b](/wiki/WASP-17b "WASP-17b"), has been found to orbit in the opposite direction to its star's rotation.[[61]](#cite_note-62) The period of one revolution of a planet's orbit is known as its [sidereal period](/wiki/Sidereal_period "Sidereal period") or *year*.[[62]](#cite_note-young-63) A planet's year depends on its distance from its star; the farther a planet is from its star, the longer the distance it must travel and the slower its speed, since it is less affected by its star's [gravity](/wiki/Gravity "Gravity").[[63]](#cite_note-64)

No planet's orbit is perfectly circular, and hence the distance of each from the host star varies over the course of its year. The closest approach to its star is called its [periastron](/wiki/Periastron "Periastron"), or [perihelion](/wiki/Perihelion "Perihelion") in the Solar System, whereas its farthest separation from the star is called its [apastron](/wiki/Apastron "Apastron") ([aphelion](/wiki/Aphelion "Aphelion")). As a planet approaches periastron, its speed increases as it trades [gravitational potential energy](/wiki/Gravitational_energy "Gravitational energy") for [kinetic energy](/wiki/Kinetic_energy "Kinetic energy"), just as a falling object on Earth accelerates as it falls. As the planet nears apastron, its speed decreases, just as an object thrown upwards on Earth slows down as it reaches the apex of its [trajectory](/wiki/Trajectory "Trajectory").[[64]](#cite_note-65)

Each planet's orbit is delineated by a set of elements:

* The *[eccentricity](/wiki/Orbital_eccentricity "Orbital eccentricity")* of an orbit describes the elongation of a planet's elliptical (oval) orbit. Planets with low eccentricities have more circular orbits, whereas planets with high eccentricities have more elliptical orbits. The planets and large moons in the Solar System have relatively low eccentricities, and thus nearly circular orbits.[[62]](#cite_note-young-63) The comets and many Kuiper belt objects, as well as several exoplanets, have very high eccentricities, and thus exceedingly elliptical orbits.[[65]](#cite_note-66)[[66]](#cite_note-67)
* The *[semi-major axis](/wiki/Semi-major_axis "Semi-major axis")* gives the size of the orbit. It is the distance from the midpoint to the longest diameter of its elliptical orbit. This distance is not the same as its apastron, because no planet's orbit has its star at its exact centre.[[62]](#cite_note-young-63)
* The *[inclination](/wiki/Inclination "Inclination")* of a planet tells how far above or below an established reference plane its orbit is tilted. In the Solar System, the reference plane is the plane of Earth's orbit, called the [ecliptic](/wiki/Ecliptic "Ecliptic"). For exoplanets, the plane, known as the *sky plane* or *plane of the sky*, is the plane perpendicular to the observer's line of sight from Earth.[[67]](#cite_note-68) The orbits of the eight major planets of the Solar System all lie very close to the ecliptic; however, some smaller objects like Pallas, Pluto, and Eris orbit at far more extreme angles to it, as do comets.[[68]](#cite_note-69) The large moons are generally not very inclined to their parent planets' [equators](/wiki/Equator "Equator"), but Earth's Moon, Saturn's Iapetus, and Neptune's Triton are exceptions. Triton is unique among the large moons in that it orbits [retrograde](/wiki/Retrograde_and_prograde_motion "Retrograde and prograde motion"), i.e. in the direction opposite to its parent planet's rotation.[[69]](#cite_note-70)
* The points at which a planet crosses above and below its reference plane are called its [ascending](/wiki/Ascending_node "Ascending node") and [descending nodes](/wiki/Descending_node "Descending node").[[62]](#cite_note-young-63) The [longitude of the ascending node](/wiki/Longitude_of_the_ascending_node "Longitude of the ascending node") is the angle between the reference plane's 0 longitude and the planet's ascending node. The [argument of periapsis](/wiki/Argument_of_periapsis "Argument of periapsis") (or perihelion in the Solar System) is the angle between a planet's ascending node and its closest approach to its star.[[62]](#cite_note-young-63)

#### Axial tilt

Main article: [Axial tilt](/wiki/Axial_tilt "Axial tilt")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/61/AxialTiltObliquity.png/250px-AxialTiltObliquity.png)](/wiki/File:AxialTiltObliquity.png)

Earth's [axial tilt](/wiki/Axial_tilt "Axial tilt") is about 23.4°. It oscillates between 22.1° and 24.5° on a 41,000-year cycle and is currently decreasing.

Planets have varying degrees of axial tilt; they spin at an angle to the [plane](/wiki/Reference_plane "Reference plane") of their stars' equators. This causes the amount of light received by each hemisphere to vary over the course of its year; when the [Northern Hemisphere](/wiki/Northern_Hemisphere "Northern Hemisphere") points away from its star, the [Southern Hemisphere](/wiki/Southern_Hemisphere "Southern Hemisphere") points towards it, and vice versa. Each planet therefore has [seasons](/wiki/Season "Season"), resulting in changes to the [climate](/wiki/Climate "Climate") over the course of its year. The time at which each hemisphere points farthest or nearest from its star is known as its [solstice](/wiki/Solstice "Solstice"). Each planet has two in the course of its orbit; when one hemisphere has its summer solstice with its day being the longest, the other has its winter solstice when its day is shortest. The varying amount of light and heat received by each hemisphere creates annual changes in weather patterns for each half of the planet. Jupiter's axial tilt is very small, so its seasonal variation is minimal; Uranus, on the other hand, has an axial tilt so extreme it is virtually on its side, which means that its hemispheres are either continually in sunlight or continually in darkness around the time of [its solstices](/wiki/Climate_of_Uranus "Climate of Uranus").[[70]](#cite_note-Weather-71) In the Solar System, Mercury, Venus, Ceres, and Jupiter have very small tilts; Pallas, Uranus, and Pluto have extreme ones; and Earth, Mars, Vesta, Saturn, and Neptune have moderate ones.[[71]](#cite_note-factsheets-72)[[72]](#cite_note-Schorghofer2016-73)[[73]](#cite_note-Carry2009-74)[[74]](#cite_note-Thomas1997b-75) Among exoplanets, axial tilts are not known for certain, though most hot Jupiters are believed to have a negligible axial tilt as a result of their proximity to their stars.[[75]](#cite_note-76) Similarly, the axial tilts of the planetary-mass moons are near zero,[[76]](#cite_note-77) with Earth's Moon at 6.687° as the biggest exception;[[77]](#cite_note-Lang2011-78) additionally, Callisto's axial tilt varies between 0 and about 2 degrees on timescales of thousands of years.[[78]](#cite_note-galileantilt-79)

#### Rotation

See also: [Exoplanetology § Rotation and axial tilt](/wiki/Exoplanetology#Rotation_and_axial_tilt "Exoplanetology")

The planets rotate around invisible axes through their centres. A planet's [rotation period](/wiki/Rotation_period "Rotation period") is known as a [stellar day](/wiki/Day "Day"). Most of the planets in the Solar System rotate in the same direction as they orbit the Sun, which is counter-clockwise as seen from above the Sun's [north pole](/wiki/Poles_of_astronomical_bodies#Geographic_poles "Poles of astronomical bodies"). The exceptions are Venus[[79]](#cite_note-80) and Uranus,[[80]](#cite_note-Belton-1984-81) which rotate clockwise, though Uranus's extreme axial tilt means there are differing conventions on which of its poles is "north", and therefore whether it is rotating clockwise or anti-clockwise.[[81]](#cite_note-82) Regardless of which convention is used, Uranus has a [retrograde rotation](/wiki/Retrograde_rotation "Retrograde rotation") relative to its orbit.[[80]](#cite_note-Belton-1984-81)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a2/Solar_system_bodies_rotation_animation.svg/250px-Solar_system_bodies_rotation_animation.svg.png)](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/a/a2/Solar_system_bodies_rotation_animation.svg)

Comparison of the rotation period (sped up 10 000 times, negative values denoting retrograde), flattening and axial tilt of the planets and the Moon [(SVG animation)](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/a/a2/Solar_system_bodies_rotation_animation.svg)

The rotation of a planet can be induced by several factors during formation. A net [angular momentum](/wiki/Angular_momentum "Angular momentum") can be induced by the individual angular momentum contributions of accreted objects. The accretion of gas by the giant planets contributes to the angular momentum. Finally, during the last stages of planet building, a [stochastic process](/wiki/Stochastic_process "Stochastic process") of protoplanetary accretion can randomly alter the spin axis of the planet.[[82]](#cite_note-araa31-83) There is great variation in the length of day between the planets, with Venus taking 243 [days](/wiki/Julian_day "Julian day") to rotate, and the giant planets only a few hours.[[83]](#cite_note-planetcompare-84) The rotational periods of exoplanets are not known, but for [hot Jupiters](/wiki/Hot_Jupiter "Hot Jupiter"), their proximity to their stars means that they are [tidally locked](/wiki/Tidal_locking "Tidal locking") (that is, their orbits are in sync with their rotations). This means, they always show one face to their stars, with one side in perpetual day, the other in perpetual night.[[84]](#cite_note-85) Mercury and Venus, the closest planets to the Sun, similarly exhibit very slow rotation: Mercury is tidally locked into a 3:2 spin–orbit resonance (rotating three times for every two revolutions around the Sun),[[85]](#cite_note-86) and Venus's rotation may be in equilibrium between [tidal forces](/wiki/Tidal_force "Tidal force") slowing it down and [atmospheric tides](/wiki/Atmospheric_tide "Atmospheric tide") created by solar heating speeding it up.[[86]](#cite_note-87)[[87]](#cite_note-88)

All the large moons are tidally locked to their parent planets;[[88]](#cite_note-89) Pluto and Charon are tidally locked to each other,[[89]](#cite_note-Young1997-90) as are Eris and Dysnomia,[[90]](#cite_note-Szakats2022-91) and probably [Orcus](/wiki/Orcus_(dwarf_planet) "Orcus (dwarf planet)") and its moon [Vanth](/wiki/Vanth_(moon) "Vanth (moon)").[[91]](#cite_note-Brown2023-92) The other dwarf planets with known rotation periods rotate faster than Earth; Haumea rotates so fast that it has been distorted into a [triaxial ellipsoid](/wiki/Triaxial_ellipsoid "Triaxial ellipsoid").[[92]](#cite_note-Rabinowitz2005-93) The exoplanet [Tau Boötis b](/wiki/Tau_Bo%C3%B6tis_b "Tau Boötis b") and its parent star [Tau Boötis](/wiki/Tau_Bo%C3%B6tis "Tau Boötis") appear to be mutually tidally locked.[[93]](#cite_note-94)[[94]](#cite_note-95)

#### Orbital clearing

Main article: [Clearing the neighbourhood](/wiki/Clearing_the_neighbourhood "Clearing the neighbourhood")

The defining dynamic characteristic of a planet, according to the IAU definition, is that it has *cleared its neighborhood*. A planet that has cleared its neighborhood has accumulated enough mass to gather up or sweep away all the [planetesimals](/wiki/Planetesimal "Planetesimal") in its orbit. In effect, it orbits its star in isolation, as opposed to sharing its orbit with a multitude of similar-sized objects. As described above, this characteristic was mandated as part of the [IAU](/wiki/International_Astronomical_Union "International Astronomical Union")'s official [definition of a planet](/wiki/2006_definition_of_planet "2006 definition of planet") in August 2006.[[2]](#cite_note-IAU-2) Although to date this criterion only applies to the Solar System, a number of young extrasolar systems have been found in which evidence suggests orbital clearing is taking place within their [circumstellar discs](/wiki/Circumstellar_disc "Circumstellar disc").[[95]](#cite_note-96)

### Physical characteristics

#### Size and shape

See also: [Earth § Size and shape](/wiki/Earth#Size_and_shape "Earth"), [Astronomical object § Shape](/wiki/Astronomical_object#Shape "Astronomical object"), and [Planetary coordinate system](/wiki/Planetary_coordinate_system "Planetary coordinate system")

Gravity causes planets to be pulled into a roughly spherical shape, so a planet's size can be expressed roughly by an average radius (for example, [Earth radius](/wiki/Earth_radius_(unit) "Earth radius (unit)") or [Jupiter radius](/wiki/Jupiter_radius "Jupiter radius")). However, planets are not perfectly spherical; for example, the [Earth's rotation](/wiki/Earth%27s_rotation "Earth's rotation") causes it to be slightly flattened at the poles with a [bulge around the equator](/wiki/Equatorial_bulge "Equatorial bulge").[[96]](#cite_note-milbert_smith96-97) Therefore, a better approximation of Earth's shape is an [oblate spheroid](/wiki/Oblate_spheroid "Oblate spheroid"), whose equatorial diameter is 43 kilometers (27 mi) larger than the [pole](/wiki/Geographical_pole "Geographical pole")-to-pole diameter.[[97]](#cite_note-ngdc2006-98) Generally, a planet's shape may be described by giving polar and equatorial radii of a [spheroid](/wiki/Spheroid "Spheroid") or specifying a [reference ellipsoid](/wiki/Reference_ellipsoid "Reference ellipsoid"). From such a specification, the planet's flattening, surface area, and volume can be calculated; its [normal gravity](/wiki/Normal_gravity "Normal gravity") can be computed knowing its size, shape, rotation rate, and mass.[[98]](#cite_note-99)

#### Mass

Main article: [Planetary mass](/wiki/Planetary_mass "Planetary mass")

A planet's defining physical characteristic is that it is massive enough for the force of its own gravity to dominate over the [electromagnetic forces](/wiki/Electromagnetic_force "Electromagnetic force") binding its physical structure, leading to a state of [hydrostatic equilibrium](/wiki/Hydrostatic_equilibrium "Hydrostatic equilibrium"). This effectively means that all planets are spherical or spheroidal. Up to a certain mass, an object can be irregular in shape, but beyond that point, which varies depending on the chemical makeup of the object, gravity begins to pull an object towards its own centre of mass until the object collapses into a sphere.[[99]](#cite_note-100)

Mass is the prime attribute by which planets are distinguished from stars. No objects between the masses of the Sun and Jupiter exist in the Solar System, but there are exoplanets of this size. The lower [stellar mass](/wiki/Stellar_mass "Stellar mass") limit is estimated to be around 75 to 80 times that of Jupiter ([MJ](/wiki/Jupiter_mass "Jupiter mass")). Some authors advocate that this be used as the upper limit for planethood, on the grounds that the internal physics of objects does not change between approximately one Saturn mass (beginning of significant self-compression) and the onset of hydrogen burning and becoming a [red dwarf](/wiki/Red_dwarf "Red dwarf") star.[[54]](#cite_note-ChenKipping-55) Beyond roughly 13 MJ (at least for objects with solar-type [isotopic abundance](/wiki/Isotopic_abundance "Isotopic abundance")), an object achieves conditions suitable for [nuclear fusion](/wiki/Nuclear_fusion "Nuclear fusion") of [deuterium](/wiki/Deuterium "Deuterium"): this has sometimes been advocated as a boundary,[[100]](#cite_note-exoworkdef-101) even though deuterium burning does not last very long and most brown dwarfs have long since finished burning their deuterium.[[58]](#cite_note-Hatzes-59) This is not universally agreed upon: the [exoplanets Encyclopaedia](/wiki/Extrasolar_Planets_Encyclopaedia "Extrasolar Planets Encyclopaedia") includes objects up to 60 MJ,[[101]](#cite_note-corot-102) and the [Exoplanet Data Explorer](/wiki/Exoplanet_Data_Explorer "Exoplanet Data Explorer") up to 24 MJ.[[102]](#cite_note-eod-103)

The smallest known exoplanet with an accurately known mass is [PSR B1257+12A](/wiki/PSR_B1257%2B12A "PSR B1257+12A"), one of the first exoplanets discovered, which was found in 1992 in orbit around a [pulsar](/wiki/Pulsar "Pulsar"). Its mass is roughly half that of the planet Mercury.[[103]](#cite_note-konacki2003-104) Even smaller is [WD 1145+017 b](/wiki/WD_1145%2B017_b "WD 1145+017 b"), orbiting a white dwarf; its mass is roughly that of the dwarf planet Haumea, and it is typically termed a minor planet.[[104]](#cite_note-105) The smallest known planet orbiting a main-sequence star other than the Sun is [Kepler-37b](/wiki/Kepler-37b "Kepler-37b"), with a mass (and radius) that is probably slightly higher than that of the Moon.[[44]](#cite_note-Barclay-2013-44) The smallest object in the Solar System generally agreed to be a geophysical planet is Saturn's moon Mimas, with a radius about 3.1% of Earth's and a mass about 0.00063% of Earth's.[[105]](#cite_note-Jacobson2022-106) Saturn's smaller moon [Phoebe](/wiki/Phoebe_(moon) "Phoebe (moon)"), currently an irregular body of 1.7% Earth's radius[[106]](#cite_note-Thomas2010-107) and 0.00014% Earth's mass,[[105]](#cite_note-Jacobson2022-106) is thought to have attained hydrostatic equilibrium and differentiation early in its history before being battered out of shape by impacts.[[107]](#cite_note-planetlike-108) Some asteroids may be fragments of [protoplanets](/wiki/Protoplanet "Protoplanet") that began to accrete and differentiate, but suffered catastrophic collisions, leaving only a metallic or rocky core today,[[108]](#cite_note-Gaffey1984-109)[[109]](#cite_note-Hardersen-Gaffey-Abell-2005-110)[[110]](#cite_note-Asphaug-Reufer-2014-111) or a reaccumulation of the resulting debris.[[111]](#cite_note-Yang2020-112)

#### Internal differentiation

Main article: [Planetary differentiation](/wiki/Planetary_differentiation "Planetary differentiation")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f7/Jupiter_interior.png/250px-Jupiter_interior.png)](/wiki/File:Jupiter_interior.png)

Illustration of the interior of Jupiter, with a rocky core overlaid by a deep layer of metallic hydrogen

Every planet began its existence in an entirely fluid state; in early formation, the denser, heavier materials sank to the centre, leaving the lighter materials near the surface. Each therefore has a [differentiated](/wiki/Planetary_differentiation "Planetary differentiation") interior consisting of a dense [planetary core](/wiki/Planetary_core "Planetary core") surrounded by a [mantle](/wiki/Mantle_(geology) "Mantle (geology)") that either is or was a [fluid](/wiki/Fluid "Fluid"). The terrestrial planets' mantles are sealed within hard [crusts](/wiki/Crust_(geology) "Crust (geology)"),[[112]](#cite_note-terrestrial-113) but in the giant planets the mantle simply blends into the upper cloud layers. The terrestrial planets have cores of elements such as [iron](/wiki/Iron "Iron") and [nickel](/wiki/Nickel "Nickel") and mantles of [silicates](/wiki/Silicate "Silicate"). Jupiter and Saturn are believed to have cores of rock and metal surrounded by mantles of [metallic hydrogen](/wiki/Metallic_hydrogen "Metallic hydrogen").[[113]](#cite_note-114) Uranus and Neptune, which are smaller, have rocky cores surrounded by mantles of water, [ammonia](/wiki/Ammonia "Ammonia"), [methane](/wiki/Methane "Methane"), and other [ices](/wiki/Volatile_(astrogeology) "Volatile (astrogeology)").[[114]](#cite_note-115) The fluid action within these planets' cores creates a [geodynamo](/wiki/Geodynamo "Geodynamo") that generates a [magnetic field](/wiki/Magnetic_field "Magnetic field").[[112]](#cite_note-terrestrial-113) Similar differentiation processes are believed to have occurred on some of the large moons and dwarf planets,[[32]](#cite_note-Grundy2019-32) though the process may not always have been completed: Ceres, Callisto, and Titan appear to be incompletely differentiated.[[115]](#cite_note-Neumann2015-116)[[116]](#cite_note-Monteux2014-117) The asteroid Vesta, though not a dwarf planet because it was battered by impacts out of roundness, has a differentiated interior[[117]](#cite_note-Vestainterior-118) similar to that of Venus, Earth, and Mars.[[110]](#cite_note-Asphaug-Reufer-2014-111)

#### Atmosphere

Main articles: [Atmosphere](/wiki/Atmosphere "Atmosphere") and [extraterrestrial atmospheres](/wiki/Extraterrestrial_atmospheres "Extraterrestrial atmospheres")

See also: [Extraterrestrial skies](/wiki/Extraterrestrial_skies "Extraterrestrial skies")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/be/Top_of_Atmosphere.jpg/250px-Top_of_Atmosphere.jpg)](/wiki/File:Top_of_Atmosphere.jpg)

Earth's atmosphere

All of the Solar System planets [except Mercury](/wiki/Atmosphere_of_Mercury "Atmosphere of Mercury")[[118]](#cite_note-119) have substantial [atmospheres](/wiki/Atmosphere "Atmosphere") because their gravity is strong enough to keep gases close to the surface. Saturn's largest moon [Titan](/wiki/Titan_(moon) "Titan (moon)") also has a substantial atmosphere thicker than that of Earth;[[119]](#cite_note-120) Neptune's largest moon [Triton](/wiki/Triton_(moon) "Triton (moon)")[[120]](#cite_note-Solar_System-121) and the dwarf planet [Pluto](/wiki/Pluto "Pluto") have more tenuous atmospheres.[[121]](#cite_note-Lellouch_2015-122) The larger giant planets are massive enough to keep large amounts of the light gases hydrogen and helium, whereas the smaller planets lose these gases into [space](/wiki/Interplanetary_medium "Interplanetary medium").[[122]](#cite_note-123) Analysis of exoplanets suggests that the threshold for being able to hold on to these light gases occurs at about 2.0+0.7  
−0.6 *M*🜨, so that Earth and Venus are near the maximum size for rocky planets.[[54]](#cite_note-ChenKipping-55)

The composition of Earth's atmosphere is different from the other planets because the various life processes that have transpired on the planet have introduced free molecular [oxygen](/wiki/Oxygen "Oxygen").[[123]](#cite_note-zeilik-124) The atmospheres of Mars and Venus are both dominated by [carbon dioxide](/wiki/Carbon_dioxide "Carbon dioxide"), but differ drastically in density: the average surface pressure of [Mars's atmosphere](/wiki/Atmosphere_of_Mars "Atmosphere of Mars") is less than 1% that of Earth's (too low to allow liquid water to exist),[[124]](#cite_note-125) while the average surface pressure of [Venus's atmosphere](/wiki/Atmosphere_of_Venus "Atmosphere of Venus") is about 92 times that of Earth's.[[125]](#cite_note-Basilevsky2003-126) It is likely that Venus's atmosphere was the result of a [runaway greenhouse effect](/wiki/Runaway_greenhouse_effect "Runaway greenhouse effect") in its history, which today makes it the hottest planet by surface temperature, hotter even than Mercury.[[126]](#cite_note-127) Despite hostile surface conditions, temperature, and pressure at about 50–55 km altitude in Venus's atmosphere are close to Earthlike conditions (the only place in the Solar System beyond Earth where this is so), and this region has been suggested as a plausible base for future [human exploration](/wiki/Space_exploration#Human_spaceflight_and_habitation "Space exploration").[[127]](#cite_note-Badescu-128) Titan has the only dense [nitrogen](/wiki/Nitrogen "Nitrogen")-rich planetary atmosphere in the Solar System other than Earth's. Just as Earth's conditions are close to the [triple point](/wiki/Triple_point "Triple point") of water, allowing it to exist in all three states on the planet's surface, so Titan's are to the triple point of [methane](/wiki/Methane "Methane").[[128]](#cite_note-129)

Planetary atmospheres are affected by the varying [insolation](/wiki/Insolation "Insolation") or internal energy, leading to the formation of dynamic [weather systems](/wiki/Weather_system "Weather system") such as [hurricanes](/wiki/Hurricane "Hurricane") (on Earth), planet-wide [dust storms](/wiki/Dust_storm "Dust storm") (on Mars), a greater-than-Earth-sized [anticyclone](/wiki/Anticyclonic_storm "Anticyclonic storm") on Jupiter (called the [Great Red Spot](/wiki/Great_Red_Spot "Great Red Spot")), and [holes in the atmosphere](/wiki/Great_Dark_Spot "Great Dark Spot") (on Neptune).[[70]](#cite_note-Weather-71) Weather patterns detected on exoplanets include a hot region on [HD 189733 b](/wiki/HD_189733_b "HD 189733 b") twice the size of the Great Red Spot,[[129]](#cite_note-knutson-130) as well as [clouds](/wiki/Cloud "Cloud") on the hot Jupiter [Kepler-7b](/wiki/Kepler-7b "Kepler-7b"),[[130]](#cite_note-ArXiv-20130930-131) the super-Earth [Gliese 1214 b](/wiki/Gliese_1214_b "Gliese 1214 b"), and others.[[131]](#cite_note-NAT-20140101a-132)[[132]](#cite_note-133)

Hot Jupiters, due to their extreme proximities to their host stars, have been shown to be losing their atmospheres into space due to stellar radiation, much like the tails of comets.[[133]](#cite_note-134)[[134]](#cite_note-135) These planets may have vast differences in temperature between their day and night sides that produce supersonic winds,[[135]](#cite_note-136) although multiple factors are involved and the details of the atmospheric dynamics that affect the day-night temperature difference are complex.[[136]](#cite_note-137)[[137]](#cite_note-138)

#### Magnetosphere

Main article: [Magnetosphere](/wiki/Magnetosphere "Magnetosphere")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/Structure_of_the_magnetosphere_LanguageSwitch.svg/250px-Structure_of_the_magnetosphere_LanguageSwitch.svg.png)](/w/index.php?title=File:Structure_of_the_magnetosphere_LanguageSwitch.svg&lang=en)

[Earth's magnetosphere](/wiki/Earth%27s_magnetic_field "Earth's magnetic field") (diagram)

One important characteristic of the planets is their intrinsic [magnetic moments](/wiki/Magnetic_moment "Magnetic moment"), which in turn give rise to magnetospheres. The presence of a magnetic field indicates that the planet is still geologically alive. In other words, magnetized planets have flows of [electrically conducting](/wiki/Electrical_conductivity "Electrical conductivity") material in their interiors, which generate their magnetic fields. These fields significantly change the interaction of the planet and solar wind. A magnetized planet creates a cavity in the solar wind around itself called the magnetosphere, which the wind cannot penetrate. The magnetosphere can be much larger than the planet itself. In contrast, non-magnetized planets have only small magnetospheres induced by interaction of the [ionosphere](/wiki/Ionosphere "Ionosphere") with the solar wind, which cannot effectively protect the planet.[[138]](#cite_note-Kivelson2007-139)

Of the eight planets in the Solar System, only Venus and Mars lack such a magnetic field.[[138]](#cite_note-Kivelson2007-139) Of the magnetized planets, the magnetic field of Mercury is the weakest and is barely able to deflect the [solar wind](/wiki/Solar_wind "Solar wind"). Jupiter's moon [Ganymede](/wiki/Ganymede_(moon) "Ganymede (moon)") has a magnetic field several times stronger, and Jupiter's is the strongest in the Solar System (so intense in fact that it poses a serious health risk to future crewed missions to all its moons inward of Callisto[[139]](#cite_note-140)). The magnetic fields of the other giant planets, measured at their surfaces, are roughly similar in strength to that of Earth, but their magnetic moments are significantly larger. The magnetic fields of Uranus and Neptune are strongly tilted relative to the planets' rotational [axes](/wiki/Axis_of_rotation "Axis of rotation") and displaced from the planets' centres.[[138]](#cite_note-Kivelson2007-139)

In 2003, a team of astronomers in Hawaii observing the star [HD 179949](/wiki/HD_179949 "HD 179949") detected a bright spot on its surface, apparently created by the magnetosphere of an orbiting hot Jupiter.[[140]](#cite_note-141)[[141]](#cite_note-142)

### Secondary characteristics

Main articles: [Natural satellite](/wiki/Natural_satellite "Natural satellite") and [ring system](/wiki/Ring_system "Ring system")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/Voyager_2_-_Saturn_Rings_-_3085_7800_2.png/250px-Voyager_2_-_Saturn_Rings_-_3085_7800_2.png)](/wiki/File:Voyager_2_-_Saturn_Rings_-_3085_7800_2.png)

The [rings of Saturn](/wiki/Rings_of_Saturn "Rings of Saturn")

Several planets or dwarf planets in the Solar System (such as Neptune and Pluto) have orbital periods that are in [resonance](/wiki/Orbital_resonance "Orbital resonance") with each other or with smaller bodies. This is common in satellite systems (e.g. the resonance between Io, [Europa](/wiki/Europa_(moon) "Europa (moon)"), and Ganymede around Jupiter, or between Enceladus and Dione around Saturn). All except Mercury and Venus have [natural satellites](/wiki/Natural_satellite "Natural satellite"), often called "moons". Earth has one, Mars has two, and the giant planets have numerous moons in complex planetary-type systems. Except for Ceres and Sedna, all the consensus dwarf planets are known to have at least one moon as well. Many moons of the giant planets have features similar to those on the terrestrial planets and dwarf planets, and some have been studied as possible abodes of life (especially Europa and Enceladus).[[142]](#cite_note-Grasset2000-143)[[143]](#cite_note-Fortes2000-144)[[144]](#cite_note-145)[[145]](#cite_note-Taubner_et_al._2018-146)[[146]](#cite_note-Affholder_et_al._2021-147)

The four giant planets are orbited by [planetary rings](/wiki/Planetary_ring "Planetary ring") of varying size and complexity. The rings are composed primarily of dust or particulate matter, but can host tiny '[moonlets](/wiki/Rings_of_Saturn#Propeller_moonlets "Rings of Saturn")' whose gravity shapes and maintains their structure. Although the origins of planetary rings are not precisely known, they are believed to be the result of natural satellites that fell below their parent planets' [Roche limits](/wiki/Roche_limit "Roche limit") and were torn apart by [tidal forces](/wiki/Tidal_force "Tidal force").[[147]](#cite_note-148)[[148]](#cite_note-149) The dwarf planets Haumea[[149]](#cite_note-Ortiz2017-150) and Quaoar also have rings.[[150]](#cite_note-Morgado2023-151)

No secondary characteristics have been observed around exoplanets. The [sub-brown dwarf](/wiki/Sub-brown_dwarf "Sub-brown dwarf") [Cha 110913−773444](/wiki/Cha_110913%E2%88%92773444 "Cha 110913−773444"), which has been described as a [rogue planet](/wiki/Rogue_planet "Rogue planet"), is believed to be orbited by a tiny [protoplanetary disc](/wiki/Protoplanetary_disc "Protoplanetary disc"),[[151]](#cite_note-Luhman-152) and the sub-brown dwarf [OTS 44](/wiki/OTS_44 "OTS 44") was shown to be surrounded by a substantial protoplanetary disk of at least 10 Earth masses.[[152]](#cite_note-joergens2013_AA558-153)

## History and etymology

Further information: [History of astronomy](/wiki/History_of_astronomy "History of astronomy") and [Timeline of Solar System astronomy](/wiki/Timeline_of_Solar_System_astronomy "Timeline of Solar System astronomy")

The idea of planets has evolved over the history of astronomy, from the divine lights of antiquity to the earthly objects of the scientific age. The concept has expanded to include worlds not only in the Solar System, but in multitudes of other extrasolar systems. The consensus as to what counts as a planet, as opposed to other objects, has changed several times. It previously encompassed [asteroids](/wiki/Asteroid "Asteroid"), [moons](/wiki/Natural_satellite "Natural satellite"), and [dwarf planets](/wiki/Dwarf_planet "Dwarf planet") like [Pluto](/wiki/Pluto "Pluto"),[[153]](#cite_note-154)[[154]](#cite_note-asteroids-155)[[155]](#cite_note-metzger22-156) and there continues to be some disagreement today.[[155]](#cite_note-metzger22-156)

### Ancient civilizations and classical planets

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/70/Apparent_retrograde_motion_of_Mars_in_2003.gif/250px-Apparent_retrograde_motion_of_Mars_in_2003.gif)](/wiki/File:Apparent_retrograde_motion_of_Mars_in_2003.gif)

The motion of 'lights' moving across the sky is the basis of the classical definition of planets: wandering stars.

The five [classical planets](/wiki/Classical_planet "Classical planet") of the [Solar System](/wiki/Solar_System "Solar System"), being visible to the naked eye, have been known since ancient times and have had a significant impact on [mythology](/wiki/Mythology "Mythology"), [religious cosmology](/wiki/Religious_cosmology "Religious cosmology"), and ancient [astronomy](/wiki/Astronomy "Astronomy"). In ancient times, astronomers noted how certain lights moved across the sky, as opposed to the "[fixed stars](/wiki/Fixed_stars "Fixed stars")", which maintained a constant relative position in the sky.[[156]](#cite_note-157) Ancient Greeks called these lights [πλάνητες](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%CF%80%CE%BB%CE%AC%CE%BD%CE%B7%CF%82 "wikt:πλάνης") [ἀστέρες](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E1%BC%80%CF%83%CF%84%CE%AE%CF%81 "wikt:ἀστήρ") (*planētes asteres*) 'wandering stars' or simply [πλανῆται](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%CF%80%CE%BB%CE%B1%CE%BD%CE%AE%CF%84%CE%B7%CF%82#Ancient_Greek "wikt:πλανήτης") (*planētai*) 'wanderers'[[157]](#cite_note-158) from which today's word "planet" was derived.[[158]](#cite_note-159)[[159]](#cite_note-160)[[160]](#cite_note-oed-161) In [ancient Greece](/wiki/Ancient_Greece "Ancient Greece"), [China](/wiki/History_of_China#Ancient_China "History of China"), [Babylon](/wiki/Babylon "Babylon"), and indeed all pre-modern civilizations,[[161]](#cite_note-162)[[162]](#cite_note-163) it was almost universally believed that Earth was the [center of the Universe](/wiki/Geocentric "Geocentric") and that all the "planets" circled Earth. The reasons for this perception were that stars and planets appeared to revolve around Earth each day[[163]](#cite_note-164) and the apparently [common-sense](/wiki/Common_sense "Common sense") perceptions that Earth was solid and stable and that it was not moving but at rest.[[164]](#cite_note-TMU-165)

#### Babylon

Main article: [Babylonian astronomy](/wiki/Babylonian_astronomy "Babylonian astronomy")

The first civilization known to have a functional theory of the planets were the [Babylonians](/wiki/Babylonia "Babylonia"), who lived in [Mesopotamia](/wiki/Mesopotamia "Mesopotamia") in the first and second millennia BC. The oldest surviving planetary astronomical text is the Babylonian [Venus tablet of Ammisaduqa](/wiki/Venus_tablet_of_Ammisaduqa "Venus tablet of Ammisaduqa"), a 7th-century BC copy of a list of observations of the motions of the planet Venus, that probably dates as early as the second millennium BC.[[165]](#cite_note-practice-166) The [MUL.APIN](/wiki/MUL.APIN "MUL.APIN") is a pair of [cuneiform](/wiki/Cuneiform "Cuneiform") tablets dating from the 7th century BC that lays out the motions of the Sun, Moon, and planets over the course of the year.[[166]](#cite_note-167) Late Babylonian astronomy is the origin of Western astronomy and indeed all Western efforts in the [exact sciences](/wiki/Exact_science "Exact science").[[167]](#cite_note-Aaboe,_Asger-168) The *[Enuma anu enlil](/wiki/Enuma_anu_enlil "Enuma anu enlil")*, written during the [Neo-Assyrian](/wiki/Neo-Assyrian "Neo-Assyrian") period in the 7th century BC,[[168]](#cite_note-169) comprises a list of [omens](/wiki/Omen "Omen") and their relationships with various celestial phenomena including the motions of the planets.[[169]](#cite_note-170)[[170]](#cite_note-ancientmes-171) The [inferior planets](/wiki/Inferior_planet "Inferior planet") [Venus](/wiki/Venus "Venus") and [Mercury](/wiki/Mercury_(planet) "Mercury (planet)") and the superior planets [Mars](/wiki/Mars "Mars"), [Jupiter](/wiki/Jupiter "Jupiter"), and [Saturn](/wiki/Saturn "Saturn") were all identified by [Babylonian astronomers](/wiki/Babylonian_astronomy "Babylonian astronomy"). These would remain the only known planets until the invention of the [telescope](/wiki/Telescope "Telescope") in early modern times.[[171]](#cite_note-172)

#### Greco-Roman astronomy

See also: [Ancient Greek astronomy](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_astronomy "Ancient Greek astronomy")

The [ancient Greeks](/wiki/Ancient_Greece "Ancient Greece") initially did not attach as much significance to the planets as the Babylonians. In the 6th and 5th centuries BC, the [Pythagoreans](/wiki/Pythagoreans "Pythagoreans") appear to have developed [their own independent planetary theory](/wiki/Pythagorean_astronomical_system "Pythagorean astronomical system"), which consisted of the Earth, Sun, Moon, and planets revolving around a "Central Fire" at the center of the Universe. [Pythagoras](/wiki/Pythagoras "Pythagoras") or [Parmenides](/wiki/Parmenides "Parmenides") is said to have been the first to identify the evening star ([Hesperos](/wiki/Hesperos "Hesperos")) and morning star ([Phosphoros](/wiki/Phosphoros "Phosphoros")) as one and the same ([Aphrodite](/wiki/Aphrodite "Aphrodite"), Greek corresponding to Latin [Venus](/wiki/Venus "Venus")),[[172]](#cite_note-burnet-173) though this had long been known in Mesopotamia.[[173]](#cite_note-Cooley-174)[[174]](#cite_note-175) In the 3rd century BC, [Aristarchus of Samos](/wiki/Aristarchus_of_Samos "Aristarchus of Samos") proposed a [heliocentric](/wiki/Heliocentrism "Heliocentrism") system, according to which Earth and the planets revolved around the Sun. The geocentric system remained dominant until the [Scientific Revolution](/wiki/Scientific_Revolution "Scientific Revolution").[[164]](#cite_note-TMU-165)

By the 1st century BC, during the [Hellenistic period](/wiki/Hellenistic_period "Hellenistic period"), the Greeks had begun to develop their own mathematical schemes for predicting the positions of the planets. These schemes, which were based on geometry rather than the arithmetic of the Babylonians, would eventually eclipse the Babylonians' theories in complexity and comprehensiveness and account for most of the astronomical movements observed from Earth with the naked eye. These theories would reach their fullest expression in the *[Almagest](/wiki/Almagest "Almagest")* written by [Ptolemy](/wiki/Ptolemy "Ptolemy") in the 2nd century CE. So complete was the domination of Ptolemy's model that it superseded all previous works on astronomy and remained the definitive astronomical text in the Western world for 13 centuries.[[165]](#cite_note-practice-166)[[175]](#cite_note-almagest-176) To the Greeks and Romans, there were seven known planets, each presumed to be [circling Earth](/wiki/Geocentrism "Geocentrism") according to the complex laws laid out by Ptolemy. They were, in increasing order from Earth (in Ptolemy's order and using modern names): the Moon, Mercury, Venus, the Sun, Mars, Jupiter, and Saturn.[[160]](#cite_note-oed-161)[[175]](#cite_note-almagest-176)[[176]](#cite_note-177)

### Medieval astronomy

Main articles: [Astronomy in the medieval Islamic world](/wiki/Astronomy_in_the_medieval_Islamic_world "Astronomy in the medieval Islamic world") and [Indian astronomy](/wiki/Indian_astronomy "Indian astronomy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/65/1660_illustration_of_Claudius_Ptolemy%27s_geocentric_model_of_the_Universe.jpg/500px-1660_illustration_of_Claudius_Ptolemy%27s_geocentric_model_of_the_Universe.jpg)](/wiki/File:1660_illustration_of_Claudius_Ptolemy%27s_geocentric_model_of_the_Universe.jpg)

1660 illustration of Claudius Ptolemy's geocentric model

After the [fall of the Western Roman Empire](/wiki/Fall_of_the_Western_Roman_Empire "Fall of the Western Roman Empire"), astronomy developed further in India and the medieval Islamic world. In 499 CE, the Indian astronomer [Aryabhata](/wiki/Aryabhata "Aryabhata") propounded a planetary model that explicitly incorporated [Earth's rotation](/wiki/Earth%27s_rotation "Earth's rotation") about its axis, which he explains as the cause of what appears to be an apparent westward motion of the stars. He also theorized that the orbits of planets were [elliptical](/wiki/Ellipse "Ellipse").[[177]](#cite_note-178) Aryabhata's followers were particularly strong in [South India](/wiki/South_India "South India"), where his principles of the diurnal rotation of Earth, among others, were followed and a number of secondary works were based on them.[[178]](#cite_note-179)

The astronomy of the [Islamic Golden Age](/wiki/Islamic_Golden_Age "Islamic Golden Age") mostly took place in the [Middle East](/wiki/Middle_East "Middle East"), [Central Asia](/wiki/Central_Asia "Central Asia"), [Al-Andalus](/wiki/Al-Andalus "Al-Andalus"), and [North Africa](/wiki/North_Africa "North Africa"), and later in the [Far East](/wiki/Far_East "Far East") and India. These astronomers, like the polymath [Ibn al-Haytham](/wiki/Ibn_al-Haytham "Ibn al-Haytham"), generally accepted geocentrism, although they did dispute Ptolemy's system of epicycles and sought alternatives. The 10th-century astronomer [Abu Sa'id al-Sijzi](/wiki/Abu_Sa%27id_al-Sijzi "Abu Sa'id al-Sijzi") accepted that the Earth rotates around its axis.[[179]](#cite_note-Scientia-180) In the 11th century, the [transit of Venus](/wiki/Transit_of_Venus "Transit of Venus") was observed by [Avicenna](/wiki/Avicenna "Avicenna").[[180]](#cite_note-181) His contemporary [Al-Biruni](/wiki/Al-Biruni "Al-Biruni") devised a method of determining the Earth's radius using [trigonometry](/wiki/Trigonometry "Trigonometry") that, unlike the older method of [Eratosthenes](/wiki/Eratosthenes "Eratosthenes"), only required observations at a single mountain.[[181]](#cite_note-The_Lost_Art_of_Finding_Our_Way-182)

### Scientific Revolution and discovery of outer planets

See also: [Heliocentrism](/wiki/Heliocentrism "Heliocentrism")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/00/The_Solar_System%2C_with_the_orbits_of_5_remarkable_comets._LOC_2013593161.jpg/250px-The_Solar_System%2C_with_the_orbits_of_5_remarkable_comets._LOC_2013593161.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Solar_System,_with_the_orbits_of_5_remarkable_comets._LOC_2013593161.jpg)

True-scale Solar System poster made by [Emanuel Bowen](/wiki/Emanuel_Bowen "Emanuel Bowen") in 1747. At that time, Uranus, Neptune, and the asteroid belts had all not yet been discovered.

With the advent of the [Scientific Revolution](/wiki/Scientific_Revolution "Scientific Revolution") and the [heliocentric model](/wiki/Heliocentric_model "Heliocentric model") of [Copernicus](/wiki/Copernicus "Copernicus"), [Galileo](/wiki/Galileo "Galileo"), and [Kepler](/wiki/Kepler "Kepler"), use of the term "planet" changed from something that moved around the sky relative to the [fixed star](/wiki/Fixed_star "Fixed star") to a body that orbited the Sun, directly (a primary planet) or indirectly (a secondary or satellite planet). Thus the Earth was added to the roster of planets,[[182]](#cite_note-galileo_project-183) and the Sun was removed. The Copernican count of primary planets stood until 1781, when [William Herschel](/wiki/William_Herschel "William Herschel") discovered [Uranus](/wiki/Uranus "Uranus").[[183]](#cite_note-Dreyer-184)

When four satellites of Jupiter (the [Galilean moons](/wiki/Galilean_moons "Galilean moons")) and five of Saturn were discovered in the 17th century, they joined Earth's Moon in the category of "satellite planets" or "secondary planets" orbiting the primary planets, though in the following decades they would come to be called simply "satellites" for short. Scientists generally considered planetary satellites to also be planets until about the 1920s, although this usage was not common among non-scientists.[[155]](#cite_note-metzger22-156)

In the first decade of the 19th century, four new "planets" were discovered: [Ceres](/wiki/Ceres_(dwarf_planet) "Ceres (dwarf planet)") (in 1801), [Pallas](/wiki/2_Pallas "2 Pallas") (in 1802), [Juno](/wiki/3_Juno "3 Juno") (in 1804), and [Vesta](/wiki/4_Vesta "4 Vesta") (in 1807). It soon became apparent that they were rather different from previously known planets: they shared the same general region of space, between Mars and Jupiter (the [asteroid belt](/wiki/Asteroid_belt "Asteroid belt")), with sometimes overlapping orbits. This was an area where only one planet had been expected, and they were much smaller than all other planets; indeed, it was suspected that they might be shards of a larger planet that had broken up. Herschel called them *[asteroids](/wiki/Asteroid "Asteroid")* (from the Greek for "starlike") because even in the largest telescopes they resembled stars, without a resolvable disk.[[154]](#cite_note-asteroids-155)[[184]](#cite_note-185)

The situation was stable for four decades, but in the 1840s several additional asteroids were discovered ([Astraea](/wiki/5_Astraea "5 Astraea") in 1845; [Hebe](/wiki/6_Hebe "6 Hebe"), [Iris](/wiki/7_Iris "7 Iris"), and [Flora](/wiki/8_Flora "8 Flora") in 1847; [Metis](/wiki/9_Metis "9 Metis") in 1848; and [Hygiea](/wiki/10_Hygiea "10 Hygiea") in 1849). New "planets" were discovered every year; as a result, astronomers began tabulating the asteroids ([minor planets](/wiki/Minor_planet "Minor planet")) separately from the major planets and assigning them numbers instead of abstract [planetary symbols](/wiki/Planetary_symbol "Planetary symbol"),[[154]](#cite_note-asteroids-155) although they continued to be considered as small planets.[[185]](#cite_note-metzger19-186)

[Neptune was discovered in 1846](/wiki/Discovery_of_Neptune "Discovery of Neptune"), its position having been predicted thanks to its gravitational influence upon Uranus. Because the orbit of Mercury appeared to be affected in a similar way, it was believed in the late 19th century that there might be [another planet even closer to the Sun](/wiki/Vulcan_(hypothetical_planet) "Vulcan (hypothetical planet)"). However, the discrepancy between Mercury's orbit and the predictions of Newtonian gravity was instead explained by an improved theory of gravity, Einstein's [general relativity](/wiki/General_relativity "General relativity").[[186]](#cite_note-187)[[187]](#cite_note-188)

[Pluto](/wiki/Pluto "Pluto") was discovered in 1930. After initial observations led to the belief that it was larger than Earth,[[188]](#cite_note-189) the object was immediately accepted as the ninth major planet. Further monitoring found the body was actually much smaller: in 1936, [Ray Lyttleton](/wiki/Raymond_Arthur_Lyttleton "Raymond Arthur Lyttleton") suggested that Pluto may be an escaped satellite of [Neptune](/wiki/Neptune "Neptune"),[[189]](#cite_note-190) and [Fred Whipple](/wiki/Fred_Lawrence_Whipple "Fred Lawrence Whipple") suggested in 1964 that Pluto may be a comet.[[190]](#cite_note-191) The discovery of its large moon [Charon](/wiki/Charon_(moon) "Charon (moon)") in 1978 showed that Pluto was only 0.2% the mass of Earth.[[191]](#cite_note-ChristyHarrington1978-192) As this was still substantially more massive than any known asteroid, and because no other [trans-Neptunian objects](/wiki/Trans-Neptunian_objects "Trans-Neptunian objects") had been discovered at that time, Pluto kept its planetary status, only officially losing it in 2006.[[192]](#cite_note-193)[[193]](#cite_note-Pluto_loses_status_as_a_planet-2006-194)

In the 1950s, [Gerard Kuiper](/wiki/Gerard_Kuiper "Gerard Kuiper") published papers on the origin of the asteroids. He recognized that asteroids were typically not spherical, as had previously been thought, and that the [asteroid families](/wiki/Asteroid_family "Asteroid family") were remnants of collisions. Thus he differentiated between the largest asteroids as "true planets" versus the smaller ones as collisional fragments. From the 1960s onwards, the term "minor planet" was mostly displaced by the term "asteroid", and references to the asteroids as planets in the literature became scarce, except for the geologically evolved largest three: Ceres, and less often Pallas and Vesta.[[185]](#cite_note-metzger19-186)

The beginning of Solar System exploration by space probes in the 1960s spurred a renewed interest in planetary science. A split in definitions regarding satellites occurred around then: planetary scientists began to reconsider the large moons as also being planets, but astronomers who were not planetary scientists generally did not.[[155]](#cite_note-metzger22-156) (This is not exactly the same as the definition used in the previous century, which classed *all* satellites as secondary planets, even non-round ones like Saturn's [Hyperion](/wiki/Hyperion_(moon) "Hyperion (moon)") or Mars's [Phobos](/wiki/Phobos_(moon) "Phobos (moon)") and [Deimos](/wiki/Deimos_(moon) "Deimos (moon)").)[[194]](#cite_note-195)[[195]](#cite_note-196) All the eight major planets and their planetary-mass moons have since been explored by spacecraft, as have many asteroids and the dwarf planets Ceres and Pluto; however, so far the only planetary-mass body beyond Earth that has been explored by humans is the Moon.[[b]](#cite_note-197)

### Defining the term *planet*

Further information: [Definition of planet](/wiki/Definition_of_planet "Definition of planet")

A growing number of astronomers argued for Pluto to be declassified as a planet, because many similar objects approaching its size had been found in the same region of the Solar System (the [Kuiper belt](/wiki/Kuiper_belt "Kuiper belt")) during the 1990s and early 2000s. Pluto was found to be just one "small" body in a population of thousands.[[196]](#cite_note-plutoplanet-198) They often referred to the demotion of the asteroids as a precedent, although that had been done based on their geophysical differences from planets rather than their being in a belt.[[155]](#cite_note-metzger22-156) Some of the larger [trans-Neptunian objects](/wiki/Trans-Neptunian_object "Trans-Neptunian object"), such as [Quaoar](/wiki/50000_Quaoar "50000 Quaoar"), [Sedna](/wiki/90377_Sedna "90377 Sedna"), [Eris](/wiki/Eris_(dwarf_planet) "Eris (dwarf planet)"), and [Haumea](/wiki/Haumea "Haumea"),[[197]](#cite_note-planeta-199) were heralded in the popular press as the [tenth planet](/wiki/Tenth_planet "Tenth planet").

The announcement of Eris in 2005, an object 27% more massive than Pluto, created the impetus for an official definition of a planet,[[196]](#cite_note-plutoplanet-198) as considering Pluto a planet would logically have demanded that Eris be considered a planet as well. Since different procedures were in place for naming planets versus non-planets, this created an urgent situation because under the rules Eris could not be named without defining what a planet was.[[155]](#cite_note-metzger22-156) At the time, it was also thought that the size required for a trans-Neptunian object to become round was about the same as that required for the moons of the giant planets (about 400 km diameter), a figure that would have suggested about 200 round objects in the Kuiper belt and thousands more beyond.[[198]](#cite_note-Brown-200)[[199]](#cite_note-BrownList-201) Many astronomers argued that the public would not accept a definition creating a large number of planets.[[155]](#cite_note-metzger22-156)

The [International Astronomical Union](/wiki/International_Astronomical_Union "International Astronomical Union")'s  
definition of a planet in the [Solar System](/wiki/Solar_System "Solar System")

> 1. Object is in [orbit](/wiki/Orbit "Orbit") around the Sun
